heroOverview: Trichinellosis is a foodborne parasitic disease caused by roundworms of the genus Trichinella, acquired almost exclusively by eating raw or undercooked meat — especially pork, wild boar, walrus, or bear — that contains encysted larvae. Within 24 hours of ingestion, larvae are released in the small intestine; adults develop and produce new larvae that migrate through the bloodstream into skeletal muscle, where they encyst and can persist for years. The CDC estimates roughly 10,000 cases occur worldwide each year, with sporadic outbreaks continuing in North America and Europe tied to game meat and home-cured pork products. Early disease resembles gastroenteritis; the classic muscle-invasion phase that follows — marked by periorbital edema, myalgia, fever, and blood eosinophilia — is highly distinctive. With prompt anthelminthic treatment and supportive care, outcomes are generally good, though severe cases can involve myocarditis, encephalitis, and rarely death.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 10,000 cases reported globally per year (CDC estimate); the true burden is higher due to underdiagnosis
keyStats.demographics: All ages affected; hunters, rural communities, and people in regions where undercooked game meat is traditional are at highest risk
keyStats.avgAge: No single peak age; outbreaks cluster in adults who share a common food source
keyStats.globalCases: Over 65,000 cases reported worldwide between 1986 and 2009 according to WHO surveillance data

definition: Trichinellosis (ICD-10: B75) is a zoonotic infection caused by nematodes of the genus Trichinella, a group of tissue-dwelling roundworms with at least 12 recognized species and genotypes. The primary species infecting humans is Trichinella spiralis, historically linked to domestic swine; T. nativa, T. britovi, T. pseudospiralis, and T. murrelli account for most cases from game animals. Humans are accidental dead-end hosts who become infected by consuming meat containing infective larvae encased in nurse-cell complexes within skeletal muscle. In the stomach and proximal small intestine, stomach acid and pepsin dissolve the cyst capsule; larvae mature into adults within 30–40 hours and begin mating. Female worms produce live newborn larvae (NBL) for 4–6 weeks; each female produces up to 1,500 NBL. NBL penetrate intestinal mucosa, enter the lymphatics and bloodstream, and disseminate to striated muscle throughout the body. The diaphragm, masseter, tongue, biceps, and intercostals are preferentially colonized because of their high blood flow. Within nurse cells, larvae coil and encyst, remaining viable for months to years. Trichinellosis falls under infectious disease and, when complicated, may also involve cardiology and neurology. The disease is reportable in many countries; in the United States, the CDC receives roughly 20 cases per year, down from hundreds annually in the mid-20th century, reflecting regulatory changes in commercial swine production.

typesClassification.description: The first 1–7 days after ingestion, when adult worms establish in the small intestine; symptoms are gastrointestinal — nausea, diarrhea, abdominal cramps — and may be mild or absent.

Begins approximately 2–8 weeks after infection as larvae migrate into and encyst in skeletal muscle; this stage produces the classic triad of periorbital edema, myalgia, and fever with marked eosinophilia.
Larvae encyst and calcify over months to years; most patients are asymptomatic, though vague muscle aches and fatigue can persist in heavy infections.

In heavy infections (> 5 larvae per gram of muscle), inflammatory infiltration of the myocardium and CNS can cause myocarditis, arrhythmias, meningitis, or encephalitis; these are uncommon but potentially fatal complications.

primarySymptoms: Periorbital or facial edema, often the most striking early sign of muscle invasion, appearing 2–3 weeks after consuming contaminated meat and affecting both eyes simultaneously.
Severe myalgia — deep, aching muscle pain affecting the limbs, masseters, tongue, and diaphragm — that worsens with movement and typically peaks 3–4 weeks after infection.
Fever, often spiking to 38–40°C, occurring as larvae disseminate through the bloodstream and elicit a systemic inflammatory response.
Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and abdominal cramping during the initial intestinal phase in the first 1–2 weeks.
Marked blood eosinophilia — eosinophils typically rise above 20% of the white cell differential and can exceed 50% in heavy infection — a hallmark laboratory finding.
Muscle weakness, particularly of the jaw (making chewing painful), the neck, and the proximal limbs.
Subconjunctival, splinter, or periungual hemorrhages from larval migration through small capillaries.
Headache and photophobia accompanying the febrile dissemination phase.
Urticarial (hive-like) skin rash in some patients, reflecting the systemic allergic response to migrating larvae.
Difficulty breathing or pleuritic chest pain when diaphragmatic or intercostal muscle involvement is heavy.
earlyWarningSigns: Diarrhea and abdominal cramping within 24–48 hours of eating game meat or home-cured pork.
A cluster of similar gastrointestinal symptoms among multiple people who shared the same meal.
Facial puffiness, especially around the eyes, developing one to two weeks after possible exposure.
New onset of diffuse muscle aches and fatigue after consuming wild game or traditionally prepared pork products.
emergencySigns: Chest pain, palpitations, or irregular heartbeat suggesting myocarditis during the larval dissemination phase.
Severe difficulty swallowing or breathing from involvement of pharyngeal, diaphragmatic, or intercostal muscles.
New neurological signs — confusion, seizures, weakness of one side — indicating encephalitis or CNS larval migration.
High fever persisting beyond two weeks despite supportive care.
Evidence of heart failure or markedly elevated cardiac enzymes (troponin, CK-MB) on blood tests.

causes.description: Traditional domestic pork and pork products — including insufficiently heated sausage, salami, and cured meats — are the leading source globally. Trichinella spiralis larvae survive low-level smoking and curing; adequate cooking (internal temperature ≥ 71°C) is the only reliable kill step.

Bear, wild boar, cougar, walrus, and other game animals are natural hosts for multiple Trichinella species. In North America, bear meat has been the predominant source of sporadic cases since commercial pork became regulated; wild boar is a major source in Europe and Asia.

Freezing kills T. spiralis in pork below a certain thickness (commercial freezing at −15°C for at least 3 weeks), but T. nativa from arctic game animals is highly freeze-resistant and survives temperatures well below −20°C for months.

Using the same knife, grinder, or cutting board for raw and cooked meat without sanitizing between uses can transfer larvae to otherwise safely prepared food, enabling low-level household transmission.

Dishes that use raw or lightly cooked minced pork — such as mett (Germany), steak tartare variants, or traditional sausage ceremonies in Southeast Asia and Eastern Europe — carry elevated risk because cooking temperatures are insufficient to destroy larvae.

causes.cause: Feeding raw meat or kitchen scraps to swine
causes.description: In countries without feed regulations, pigs fed garbage or raw meat scraps maintain the domestic swine–human transmission cycle. Regulations banning garbage feeding to pigs in the United States sharply reduced commercial pork-associated cases from the 1960s onward.

riskFactors.factor: Eating wild game or non-commercially inspected pork
riskFactors.description: Home-slaughtered pigs and wild game are not subject to routine Trichinella testing; consuming these without thorough cooking at ≥ 71°C is the principal modifiable risk factor in high-income countries.

riskFactors.factor: Residence in or travel to endemic regions
riskFactors.description: Trichinellosis is endemic in parts of Eastern Europe, Southeast Asia, Latin America, and sub-Arctic indigenous communities; travelers who consume local traditional meat dishes face elevated exposure.
Hunters who process and share game meat face repeated exposure; community outbreaks in hunting groups are well documented in North America and Europe.

Cold-smoking, air-drying, and salt-curing at home rarely reach temperatures or conditions sufficient to inactivate Trichinella larvae reliably; switching to tested or commercially processed products reduces risk.

People with HIV infection, organ transplant recipients, or those on long-term corticosteroids may develop more severe or atypical disease, as the cellular immune response contributes to larval clearance.

riskFactors.factor: Young age and heavy worm burden
riskFactors.description: The severity of trichinellosis correlates with the number of larvae ingested (inoculum size); children who eat large portions relative to body weight can experience more intense symptoms.

affectedDemographics: Hunters and their families who share wild game meals
Residents of Eastern Europe, Southeast Asia, and sub-Arctic communities where traditional pork and game preparations are common
Travelers to endemic regions who consume locally prepared meat dishes
Farming and rural communities in countries with unregulated swine feed practices
Indigenous populations in Arctic and sub-Arctic regions consuming walrus and seal meat
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of trichinellosis rests on the combination of compatible clinical findings, epidemiological exposure to at-risk meat, and laboratory confirmation. The classic clinical picture — periorbital edema, fever, diffuse myalgia, and peripheral eosinophilia above 500 cells/μL — following a known at-risk meal is strongly suggestive. Serological testing using enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) for anti-Trichinella IgG antibodies is the primary confirmatory method; antibodies typically become detectable 2–4 weeks after infection and remain elevated for months. A Western blot assay using excretory-secretory antigens confirms positive ELISA results in uncertain cases. Blood eosinophilia and elevated muscle enzymes — creatine kinase (CK), lactate dehydrogenase (LDH), and aldolase — support the diagnosis. Muscle biopsy of the deltoid or gastrocnemius demonstrating encysted larvae provides definitive histological proof, but it is usually reserved for cases where serology is non-diagnostic. Direct examination of the implicated meat using pepsin digestion or artificial digestion followed by trichinoscopy can identify larvae in the food source and is epidemiologically useful for outbreak investigations. In neurological or cardiac complications, lumbar puncture and cardiac monitoring supplement standard testing. The key differential diagnoses include eosinophilic myositis from other causes, toxoplasmosis, viral myositis, and hypersensitivity syndromes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects specific IgG antibodies to Trichinella excretory-secretory antigens as the primary serological confirmation test.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw from the arm; results typically require 1–3 days in a reference laboratory. Sensitivity exceeds 95% when the test is performed ≥ 3 weeks after exposure; earlier testing may be falsely negative and should be repeated.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms positive or equivocal ELISA results using Trichinella excretory-secretory antigen bands, distinguishing true infection from cross-reactive antibodies.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed on the same serum sample as the ELISA; results take several additional days at a specialized laboratory.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the eosinophilia that is virtually universal in symptomatic trichinellosis, typically peaking at 20–50% of the WBC differential during larval migration.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood test; results within hours. Eosinophil count above 500/μL in the right context strongly supports the diagnosis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Reflects myocyte damage from burrowing larvae; elevated enzymes correlate with disease severity and can track treatment response.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Routine blood draw; CK may be markedly elevated (> 1,000 IU/L) during active myositis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides definitive histological proof by identifying coiled Trichinella larvae in nurse-cell complexes within skeletal muscle fibers.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small sample (usually 1–2 cm) is taken under local anesthetic from the deltoid or gastrocnemius. The tissue is either examined fresh by compression or sent for formalin-fixed histology; results take 1–5 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Epidemiologically confirms the source of an outbreak by recovering viable larvae from the implicated meat sample.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed in a laboratory on leftover meat from the index meal; not a patient-facing test but important for outbreak confirmation and public health reporting.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects myocarditis-related arrhythmias, conduction defects, or wall-motion abnormalities in patients with chest symptoms or elevated troponin.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An ECG is non-invasive and takes minutes; echocardiography takes 30–60 minutes and involves a probe on the chest wall.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of trichinellosis depends on the phase of infection and disease severity. Anthelmintic therapy is most effective during the intestinal phase, when adult worms are still present in the gut, and should be started as soon as possible after diagnosis or strong clinical suspicion. Albendazole (400 mg twice daily for 8–14 days) is the drug of first choice and has superseded mebendazole in most guidelines; it penetrates muscle tissue better and achieves higher systemic drug levels. Mebendazole (200–400 mg three times daily for 13–15 days) is an acceptable alternative. Once larvae are fully encysted in muscle — generally after 4–6 weeks — anthelmintic efficacy against the larval stage decreases substantially, though treatment is still offered to reduce adult worm burden in the rare event of ongoing intestinal infection. Corticosteroids (prednisone 30–60 mg/day, tapering over 1–2 weeks) are added for moderate-to-severe disease with significant myositis, myocarditis, or neurological involvement, to suppress the potentially tissue-damaging immune response; they should not be used without simultaneous anthelmintics because steroids can prolong adult worm survival. Supportive care includes analgesics for myalgia, antipyretics for fever, and close monitoring of cardiac and neurological status in severe cases. Most patients with mild-to-moderate disease recover fully within 3–6 months without lasting organ damage. Treatment of close contacts who shared the same meal is recommended even if asymptomatic.

medicalTreatments.name: Albendazole
medicalTreatments.description: A benzimidazole anthelmintic given at 400 mg orally twice daily for 8–14 days; it inhibits microtubule polymerization in the worm, disrupting glucose uptake and energy metabolism. It is better absorbed than mebendazole and penetrates muscle more effectively. Taken with a fatty meal to improve absorption.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Parasitological cure rates approach 80–90% when treatment begins in the intestinal phase (first week); efficacy against encysted muscle larvae after 4–6 weeks is lower, estimated at 40–60%.

medicalTreatments.name: Mebendazole
medicalTreatments.description: An alternative benzimidazole given at 200–400 mg three times daily for 13–15 days; approved in many countries and widely available. Less bioavailable than albendazole due to poor absorption, limiting its effect on muscle-phase larvae.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical improvement in approximately 70–85% of intestinal-phase cases; muscle-phase efficacy is modest, though symptom duration may be shortened.

medicalTreatments.name: Corticosteroids (prednisone or prednisolone)
medicalTreatments.description: Added at 30–60 mg/day, tapering over 1–2 weeks, for moderate-to-severe trichinellosis with significant myositis, myocarditis, or neurological signs. Suppress the host inflammatory response that contributes to tissue damage. Always co-prescribed with anthelminthic therapy to avoid prolonging worm survival.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces duration and severity of the inflammatory myositis phase in severe disease; no randomised controlled trial data, but observational evidence supports use in cardiac and neurological complications.

medicalTreatments.name: Analgesics and NSAIDs
medicalTreatments.description: Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs or paracetamol/acetaminophen are used to manage the intense myalgia and fever; NSAIDs provide additional anti-inflammatory benefit during the muscle phase.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Provide clinically meaningful pain relief in the majority of patients with mild-to-moderate myalgia; do not affect parasite burden but reduce symptom burden significantly.

alternativeTreatments.name: Adequate cooking and food safety measures
alternativeTreatments.description: Cooking all pork and game meat to an internal temperature of at least 71°C (160°F) using a meat thermometer is the most effective intervention and prevents infection entirely; no alternative therapy replaces this primary prevention.

alternativeTreatments.name: High-dose vitamin C (adjunctive)
alternativeTreatments.description: Some practitioners have used high-dose ascorbic acid as an adjunctive anti-inflammatory measure in the convalescent phase based on theoretical antioxidant effects; no controlled trial evidence supports this as a stand-alone intervention.

whySeeSpecialist: An infectious disease specialist should be involved whenever trichinellosis is confirmed or strongly suspected, both to guide anthelmintic therapy and to report the case to public health authorities for outbreak investigation. Cardiac involvement — suspected myocarditis, arrhythmia, or elevated troponin — warrants cardiology consultation. Neurological complications require a neurologist. The infectious disease specialist can also coordinate testing of close contacts who shared the implicated meat and liaise with the health department to identify and recall any remaining contaminated product.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for an infectious disease physician with experience in tropical or parasitic diseases. In areas where trichinellosis is rare, academic medical centres with tropical medicine programs or CDC Parasitic Disease consultants are excellent resources. For cardiac or neurological complications, choose a team-based approach combining infectious disease, cardiology, and neurology. Ask whether the physician has access to serological testing at a reference laboratory and whether they routinely report cases to public health.
preventionStrategies: Cook all pork, wild boar, bear, and other game meat to an internal temperature of at least 71°C (160°F); use a meat thermometer, not color or texture as a guide.
Freeze commercially produced pork at −15°C for at least 3 weeks to kill T. spiralis; do not rely on home freezing for wild game, as arctic species such as T. nativa are freeze-resistant.
Avoid raw or lightly cured meat products, including home-made sausages, salami, or dishes that call for raw minced pork.
Prevent cross-contamination by using separate cutting boards and utensils for raw meat and other foods, and washing hands thoroughly after handling raw meat.
Support and comply with regulatory requirements for commercial swine feed (prohibiting garbage and raw animal byproducts) that have dramatically reduced swine-derived cases.
When traveling to endemic regions, avoid traditional raw or undercooked meat dishes and choose heat-treated or commercially produced alternatives.
lifestyleModifications: During the acute phase, rest and limit strenuous physical activity, especially if muscle enzymes are markedly elevated or cardiac involvement is suspected.
Stay well hydrated to support kidney clearance during systemic larval migration.
Complete the full course of prescribed anthelmintic therapy even when symptoms begin to improve, typically 8–14 days.
Hunter households should process wild game in separate dedicated equipment that is cleaned thoroughly before use for other foods.
Inform family members or hunting companions who shared the same meat so they can seek evaluation and prophylactic treatment if needed.
Report suspected trichinellosis to the local health department; outbreak source identification protects others.

recommended: Well-cooked meats confirmed to have reached at least 71°C internal temperature
High-protein, nutrient-dense foods during recovery to support muscle repair
Adequate hydration with water and electrolyte-containing fluids during the febrile phase
Soft, easy-to-chew foods if masseteric myalgia makes chewing painful
avoid: Raw or undercooked pork, wild boar, bear, walrus, horse, and other game meats
Home-cured sausages and salami made from uninspected pork or game
Raw meat dishes (mett, steak tartare made with pork) even from artisan sources
Foods prepared with the same equipment used for raw game meat without thorough cleaning

exerciseGuidelines: Physical rest is advised during the febrile and acute myositis phases of trichinellosis, as exertion worsens myalgia and, in cardiac involvement, risks arrhythmia. Once fever has resolved and CK levels are normalizing — typically 4–6 weeks after treatment — gentle walking and stretching can be reintroduced. A return to full physical activity should follow medical clearance, particularly if there was any cardiac involvement during the acute phase.
prognosis: The prognosis for trichinellosis is generally good in mild and moderate cases, with full recovery expected over 3–6 months after effective treatment. Case fatality rates in modern reported series are well below 1% when medical care is available. Severe cases complicated by myocarditis, encephalitis, or respiratory muscle failure carry a higher mortality risk — historically up to 5–10% in outbreak settings before modern intensive care — but this is uncommon today. Residual muscle discomfort or fatigue can persist for months in heavy infections due to retained calcified cysts, but these do not usually impair function long-term. Eosinophilia and serology may remain elevated for years after clinical recovery, which does not indicate active disease.
recoveryTimeline: Intestinal symptoms typically resolve within 1–2 weeks regardless of treatment. With timely anthelmintic therapy, muscle-phase symptoms — periorbital edema and myalgia — peak at 3–4 weeks and generally begin improving within 1–2 weeks of starting treatment. Most patients feel substantially better within 6–8 weeks. Elevated CK and eosinophilia normalize over 2–3 months. Calcification of muscle larvae takes several months and marks the transition to the chronic quiescent phase. Cardiac and neurological complications require longer recovery of weeks to months under specialist monitoring.
complications: Myocarditis — larval migration through the myocardium causes inflammation that can result in arrhythmias, heart block, congestive heart failure, and, in rare cases, sudden cardiac death; troponin and ECG monitoring are essential in moderate-to-severe disease.
Encephalitis and meningitis — CNS larval migration can cause headache, confusion, seizures, focal neurological deficits, or coma; cerebrospinal fluid pleocytosis and eosinophilia may be found.
Respiratory failure — heavy involvement of the diaphragm and intercostal muscles can cause hypoventilation, requiring supplemental oxygen or mechanical ventilation in severe cases.
Thromboembolic events — trichinellosis can trigger a hypercoagulable state with venous thrombosis and, rarely, arterial embolism, attributed to larval emboli and endothelial inflammation.
Secondary bacterial infection — debilitated patients with heavy worm burden are susceptible to opportunistic bacterial infections, including pneumonia.
Chronic muscle pain and calcified cysts — encysted and calcified larvae in skeletal muscle can produce persistent low-grade myalgia and radiographically detectable calcifications for years.

supportResources.name: CDC — Trichinellosis
supportResources.description: Patient and clinician information on trichinellosis symptoms, diagnosis, treatment, and prevention from the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.

supportResources.name: WHO — Trichinellosis fact sheet
supportResources.description: Global epidemiology, risk factors, and prevention guidance from the World Health Organization.

supportResources.name: NIDDK — Foodborne Illnesses and Germs
supportResources.description: Plain-English overview of foodborne illnesses including parasitic infections, with guidance on when to seek care.

sources.title: Pozio E, Murrell KD. Systematics and epidemiology of Trichinella. Advances in Parasitology, 2006
Gottstein B et al. Epidemiology, Diagnosis, Treatment, and Control of Trichinellosis. Clinical Microbiology Reviews, 2009
Bruschi F, Murrell KD. New aspects of human trichinellosis: the impact of new Trichinella species. Postgraduate Medical Journal, 2002
USDA Food Safety and Inspection Service — Trichinella and Pork
Murrell KD, Pozio E. Worldwide occurrence and impact of human trichinellosis, 1986–2009. Emerging Infectious Diseases, 2011

confusedWithConditions.name: Strongyloidiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Strongyloidiasis is also a tissue-invasive nematode infection causing eosinophilia and GI symptoms, but lacks the periorbital edema and masseter myalgia of trichinellosis; diagnosis is by stool exam or serology for Strongyloides.

confusedWithConditions.name: Schistosomiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acute schistosomiasis (Katayama fever) also presents with fever, urticaria, and eosinophilia but is acquired through fresh water exposure in endemic regions, not meat consumption, and lacks the characteristic periorbital swelling.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hookworm infection
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hookworm causes iron-deficiency anemia and eosinophilia but produces cutaneous entry signs (ground itch) and lacks the acute febrile myositis and periorbital edema of trichinellosis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Leishmaniasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Visceral leishmaniasis shares fever and systemic illness but causes splenomegaly and pancytopenia rather than eosinophilia and myalgia, and is vector-borne, not foodborne.

relatedConditions.name: Ancylostomiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another soil-transmitted nematode infection causing eosinophilia and systemic illness; differentiated by route of infection (skin penetration vs. meat ingestion) and clinical features.

relatedConditions.name: Necatoriasis
relatedConditions.relevance: A closely related hookworm infection transmitted via skin contact with contaminated soil; shares eosinophilia and anemia with trichinellosis but has a distinct life cycle.

relatedConditions.name: Trichuriasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another intestinal roundworm infection endemic in tropical regions; shares fecal-oral transmission and nutritional complications with other soil-transmitted helminths.

relatedConditions.name: Ascariasis
relatedConditions.relevance: The most prevalent soil-transmitted helminthiasis worldwide; its larval migration phase can produce pulmonary symptoms, and co-infection with Trichinella may occur in endemic settings.

relatedConditions.name: Strongyloidiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: A tissue-invasive nematode that, like Trichinella, can disseminate in immunocompromised individuals; both may require anthelminthic therapy with similar agents.

faqs.question: What is trichinellosis and how do you get it?
faqs.answer: Trichinellosis is a parasitic infection caused by Trichinella roundworms, acquired by eating raw or undercooked meat — most often pork, wild boar, or bear — containing larval cysts. Within hours of eating contaminated meat, larvae are released, mature into adult worms in the intestine, and produce offspring that migrate into skeletal muscle.

faqs.question: What are the first signs of trichinellosis?
faqs.answer: The earliest symptoms appear 1–7 days after eating contaminated meat and include nausea, diarrhea, and abdominal cramping. These are followed 2–3 weeks later by the hallmark muscle-invasion signs: swelling around both eyes, deep muscle pain, and fever with high eosinophils on a blood test.

faqs.question: Is trichinellosis contagious from person to person?
faqs.answer: No. Trichinellosis is not contagious between people. It is acquired only by eating meat that contains Trichinella larvae. A person with the infection cannot spread it to others through casual contact, respiratory droplets, or shared utensils.

faqs.question: How is trichinellosis treated?
faqs.answer: The first-line treatment is albendazole 400 mg twice daily for 8–14 days, started as soon as possible after diagnosis. Corticosteroids are added for severe myositis or cardiac involvement. Most patients with mild to moderate disease recover fully with treatment; muscle pain may persist for several weeks.

faqs.question: How long does it take to recover from trichinellosis?
faqs.answer: Intestinal symptoms resolve within 1–2 weeks. Muscle pain and swelling around the eyes typically peak at 3–4 weeks and improve gradually over 6–8 weeks with treatment. Elevated blood markers may remain high for 2–3 months. Full recovery is expected within 3–6 months in uncomplicated cases.

faqs.question: Can trichinellosis be fatal?
faqs.answer: Trichinellosis is rarely fatal with modern medical care. Case fatality rates in recent reported series are below 1%. Death is most likely in heavy infections complicated by myocarditis, respiratory muscle failure, or encephalitis. Prompt anthelminthic and supportive treatment dramatically reduces this risk.

faqs.question: What blood test findings suggest trichinellosis?
faqs.answer: The most characteristic laboratory finding is a marked increase in eosinophils, often above 20% of the white blood cell count. Muscle enzymes (creatine kinase, LDH) are also elevated. Specific blood antibody tests (ELISA and Western blot) confirm the diagnosis but may be negative in the first two weeks.

faqs.question: What temperature kills Trichinella in meat?
faqs.answer: Cooking pork or game meat to an internal temperature of at least 71°C (160°F) reliably kills Trichinella larvae. Using a meat thermometer is essential; color and texture are unreliable guides. Freezing kills T. spiralis in thin pork cuts at −15°C for 3 weeks, but arctic Trichinella species in wild game are freeze-resistant.

faqs.question: Is wild game meat safe to eat?
faqs.answer: Wild game meat can be safe if cooked thoroughly to 71°C (160°F) measured with a meat thermometer. Freezing game meat does not reliably kill all Trichinella species, particularly in bear and arctic animals. When in doubt about the safety of game meat, prioritize thorough cooking.

faqs.question: Why does trichinellosis cause swelling around the eyes?
faqs.answer: The characteristic periorbital edema occurs during the muscle-invasion phase because migrating larvae trigger an intense immune reaction with fluid accumulation in soft tissues. The loose connective tissue around the eyes is particularly susceptible to this inflammatory swelling. It usually affects both eyes simultaneously and is one of the most distinctive features of trichinellosis.

faqs.question: Does trichinellosis cause permanent muscle damage?
faqs.answer: In most cases, no permanent damage occurs. Larvae eventually calcify in muscle and become inactive. In heavy infections, persistent low-grade muscle aches and radiological calcifications can remain, but these rarely cause functional impairment. Cardiac complications, if not treated early, can result in lasting heart damage in a minority of patients.

faqs.question: Should family members who ate the same meat be tested?
faqs.answer: Yes. Anyone who ate the same implicated meat should be evaluated even if asymptomatic, because preventive anthelmintic treatment during the intestinal phase (within the first week) can abort the infection before muscle invasion. Public health authorities should also be notified to trace and recall the remaining contaminated food source.

faqs.question: Can you get trichinellosis from beef?
faqs.answer: Beef is not a recognized source of Trichinella infection under normal circumstances because cattle are not natural hosts. Almost all human cases are linked to pork, wild boar, bear, walrus, horse, or other game meats. If beef is mixed with pork (as in some sausages), the risk follows the pork component.

faqs.question: Is commercial pork safe from trichinellosis today?
faqs.answer: In countries with regulated commercial pork production — including the United States, Canada, and the European Union — commercially raised pork is considered very low risk because of feed regulations that ban feeding raw meat scraps to pigs. The majority of current cases in the U.S. involve wild game or non-commercially produced pork.

faqs.question: What happens if trichinellosis affects the heart?
faqs.answer: Cardiac trichinellosis causes myocarditis — inflammation of the heart muscle — which can produce arrhythmias, heart block, chest pain, and, in severe cases, heart failure. Electrocardiogram changes and elevated troponin levels indicate cardiac involvement. Corticosteroids are used alongside anthelmintics, and cardiology monitoring is essential.

faqs.question: Can you be reinfected with Trichinella?
faqs.answer: Yes, prior infection does not confer lasting protective immunity. A person can be infected multiple times if they continue to eat improperly prepared meat containing Trichinella larvae. Strict food safety practices remain important after a first episode.

faqs.question: How common is trichinellosis in the United States?
faqs.answer: Trichinellosis is now rare in the U.S., with approximately 20 cases reported to the CDC per year. Cases have declined sharply since the 1940s due to regulations on commercial swine feed and better food safety education. Most current U.S. cases are associated with wild game such as bear or wild boar.

faqs.question: Does smoking or curing meat kill Trichinella?
faqs.answer: Not reliably. Traditional smoking and curing at home rarely reach temperatures high enough to kill Trichinella larvae throughout the meat. Cold-smoking and air-drying are particularly unreliable. Only thorough cooking to at least 71°C (160°F) internal temperature is dependably effective.

faqs.question: What specialist should I see for trichinellosis?
faqs.answer: An infectious disease specialist is the primary expert for diagnosis, anthelmintic treatment, and public health reporting. If heart symptoms develop, a cardiologist should be consulted. Neurological complications require a neurologist. In countries where the disease is uncommon, a tropical medicine physician at an academic center may also be helpful.

faqs.question: How does the doctor confirm the diagnosis of trichinellosis?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is confirmed with a blood antibody test (ELISA for anti-Trichinella IgG), which becomes positive 2–4 weeks after infection. Supporting findings include high eosinophils and elevated muscle enzymes on blood tests. In uncertain cases, a small muscle biopsy from the deltoid or calf can demonstrate encysted larvae under the microscope.

metaDescription: Trichinellosis is a parasitic infection from undercooked pork or game meat causing muscle pain, eye swelling and fever. Learn symptoms and treatment options.
